Use Case Guide

Construction Software for Electrical Contractors

Electrical contractors need different tools for service work vs. commercial installation. Here's what to use based on how your business operates.

✓ Updated April 2026

Electrical contractors run two fundamentally different types of work: service and repair (residential panels, outlets, troubleshooting, small installs) and project-based installation (new construction wiring, commercial tenant improvements, large renovations). The software tools that work well for one often fall short for the other.

Software by electrical work type

Residential electrical service work

For electrical contractors whose primary revenue is residential service calls — troubleshooting, panel upgrades, EV charger installations, outlet and switch work — the key software features are scheduling, dispatching, quoting, and invoicing. Fast quote turnaround and efficient scheduling of multiple technicians are the operational priorities.

Best picks:

  • Jobber ($69/mo): Clean scheduling, fast quote creation, online payment collection. Sufficient for most small residential electrical service businesses.
  • Contractor Foreman ($49/mo): More features including time tracking and job costing. Better for contractors who need both service and installation tools in one platform.

Commercial electrical subcontracting

Commercial electrical subcontractors — working under a GC, billing with AIA pay applications, managing projects over weeks or months — have completely different software needs. The key requirements are AIA billing (G702/G703), job costing against bid, change order tracking, and subcontractor compliance documentation.

Best pick: Knowify (~$149/mo) — The only platform in the mid-market specifically designed for commercial subcontractors. AIA billing, contract management, and job costing are all native. Integrates with QuickBooks for accounting.

Large residential electrical installation

Electrical contractors doing large residential projects — full home wiring for custom builds, complete electrical renovations — need project management tools that handle multi-week timelines, subcontractor coordination (low-voltage subs, inspection scheduling), and client communication.

Best pick: Buildertrend ($499/mo) — Full project management for complex residential installations. Scheduling, client portal, change orders, and document management in one platform.

Buildertrend — When your electrical projects run for weeks and involve multiple trades.

Visit Buildertrend →

Commercial electrical estimating: a separate category

Assembly-based electrical estimating — building bids from labor units, material databases, and electrical assemblies — requires specialized tools beyond what general construction software offers. If commercial estimating is a core part of your business, evaluate:

  • Accubid (Trimble): Industry standard for commercial electrical estimating. Complex but comprehensive.
  • McCormick Estimating: Strong alternative with good labor unit databases.
  • ConEst: Purpose-built for electrical estimating with built-in labor units.

These pair with a separate PM platform (Knowify for subs, Buildertrend or Procore for larger operations) for actual project execution.

Key features for electrical contractors

  • AIA billing (if doing commercial work)
  • Job costing by cost code — labor, material, subcontractor separately
  • Change order tracking with customer or GC approval workflow
  • Scheduling — crew dispatch for service or multi-trade coordination for installs
  • Time tracking — field hours allocated to specific jobs and cost codes
  • Subcontractor compliance (certificates of insurance, lien waivers)
  • Mobile access — electricians need quick job notes and time entry from their phones
